description:
Use of the `empty` construct.
>Ambiguous check due to `empty()` loose semantic

`empty()` exhibits unexpected behavior on specific value.
It is unclear what condition is being treated with `empty()`.

Help: Use strict comparison or specific predicate function to clearly convey your intent.

Use of the `isset` construct.
>Ambiguous check due to `isset()` conflating existence and null comparison

`isset()` returns false for both unset variables and null values.
It is unclear whether you're checking for variable existence or non-null value.

Help: Use explicit null comparison (`!== null`) or the null coalescing operator (`??`).

Use of the `empty` construct.
>Ambiguous check due to `empty()` loose semantic

`empty()` exhibits unexpected behavior on specific value.
It is unclear what condition is being treated with `empty()`.

Help: Use strict comparison or specific predicate function to clearly convey your intent.

Unsafe use of `$GLOBALS` variable.
>The `$GLOBALS` variable is used here

Accessing the `$GLOBALS` array directly can lead to similar issues as using the `global` keyword.
It can make your code harder to understand, test, and maintain due to the implicit global state.
Consider using dependency injection or other techniques to manage state and avoid relying on global variables.

Help: Refactor your code to avoid using the `$GLOBALS` variable directly.

Consider using early continue pattern to reduce nesting.
>This if statement wraps the entire loop body
>The loop can benefit from early continue to improve readability

This pattern improves readability by reducing nesting levels.

Help: Invert the condition and use `continue` to exit early, then place the main logic outside the if block.

Use of the `empty` construct.
>Ambiguous check due to `empty()` loose semantic

`empty()` exhibits unexpected behavior on specific value.
It is unclear what condition is being treated with `empty()`.

Help: Use strict comparison or specific predicate function to clearly convey your intent.

Class has a high kan defect score.
>Class has a kan defect score of 2.04, which exceeds the threshold of 1.6.

Kan defect is a heuristic used by phpmetrics to estimate defect-proneness based on control-flow statements.

Help: You can also consider splitting large units of code into smaller, more focused units.

Use of the `isset` construct.
>Ambiguous check due to `isset()` conflating existence and null comparison

`isset()` returns false for both unset variables and null values.
It is unclear whether you're checking for variable existence or non-null value.

Help: Use explicit null comparison (`!== null`) or the null coalescing operator (`??`).

Help: Use explicit null comparison (`!== null`) or the null coalescing operator (`??`).

Parameter list is too long.
>This list has 9 parameters, which exceeds the threshold of 5.

Having a large number of parameters can make functions harder to understand and maintain.

Help: Try reducing the number of parameters, or consider passing an object or a shape instead.

Class has high complexity.
>Class has a cyclomatic complexity of 18, which exceeds the threshold of 15.

Unbraced variable in string interpolation.
>String interpolation contains unbraced variables
>Variable should be enclosed in curly braces

Using curly braces around variables in interpolated strings improves readability and prevents potential parsing issues.

Help: Wrap the variable in curly braces, e.g., `{$variable}`.

Class has high complexity.
>Class has a cyclomatic complexity of 76, which exceeds the threshold of 15.

Class has a high kan defect score.
>Class has a kan defect score of 5.07, which exceeds the threshold of 1.6.

Kan defect is a heuristic used by phpmetrics to estimate defect-proneness based on control-flow statements.

Help: You can also consider splitting large units of code into smaller, more focused units.

Class has too many methods.
>Class has 22 methods, which exceeds the threshold of 10.

Having a large number of methods can make structures harder to understand and maintain.

Help: Try reducing the number of methods, or consider splitting the structure into smaller, more focused structures.

Class has too many properties.
>Class has 18 properties, which exceeds the threshold of 10.

Having a large number of properties can make classes harder to understand and maintain.

Help: Try reducing the number of properties, or consider grouping related properties into a single object.

Avoid `else` clauses.
>This `else` clause can often be eliminated

Code is often clearer when the main logic is not nested inside an `if` statement.

Help: Consider refactoring to use an early return (a guard clause) to simplify the control flow.

Consider using dependency injection or other techniques to manage state and avoid relying on global variables.

Avoid multiple assignments in a single statement.
>This chained assignment can be confusing

Multiple assignments can obscure the author's intent and may be confused with a comparison.

Help: Did you mean `$newValue = ($ruleId == 'CA' . $caId)`? If not, split this into separate assignment statements for clarity.

Avoid boolean flag parameters.

Help: Refactor by splitting the function into two separate methods, each with a clear, descriptive name.

Use identity comparison `===` instead of equality comparison `==`.
>Equality operator is used here

Identity comparison `===` checks for both value and type equality, while equality comparison `==` performs type coercion, which can lead to unexpected results.

Help: Use `===` to ensure both value and type are equal.

Avoid assignments in conditions.
>This is an assignment
>This is the condition

Assigning a value within a condition can lead to unexpected behavior and make the code harder to read and understand.
It's easy to confuse assignment (`=`) with comparison (`==`) in this context. Ensure you're using the correct operator.
